title: Raise Your English Level with More Vocabulary
summary: Some studies have shown that for learners to fully understand a written work, they need to know 95 percent or more of the words in it. That means that if there are only five or more words you do not know in a 100-word text, you might not really understand it!

summary: Writing a good email can mean the difference between someone reading it and getting a response and someone ignoring it.

title: Students with Disabilities Praise Assistive Technology Using AI

summary: A good way to start the new year is setting reasonable goals for learning English. Learn how goals help guide language learners and give them reasons to continue studying a language.

title: In Uganda, Cost of Attending School Keeps Children Home
summary: The World Bank says African countries south of the Sahara Desert have the highest rates of children out of school. In Uganda, parents say school costs are high, and they increase for many reasons. Schools regularly ask for additional money to pay for different projects.

title: In Uganda, Cost of Attending School Keeps Children Home
Learning How to Ask Questions
summary: Suzanne Meyer says that students need to practice question-asking activities. Question-asking activities help students to become better language learners. By practicing question-asking activities, students can improve their language skills and develop critical thinking skills.

title: Tips for Better Writing
summary: Everyone struggles with writing, however there are ways to ease your struggle when working on an essay or paper for class. Jennifer Ahern-Dodson, assistant professor of the Practice in Writing Studies at Duke University is here with some tips.

title: Improve Your Pronunciation By 'Shadowing' Others
summary: Many English learners who want to improve the way they pronounce words often think about how to make specific sounds. But English learners can better improve their pronunciation by shadowing – matching the rise and fall of a native speaker’s voice.

title: Learn about US Presidential Campaigns
summary: Every four years, the United States holds an election for the President in November. In the summer before the election, the political parties meet to nominate their candidates and tell the voters about their policies. Use this to learn or teach the expressions you might hear during an election year.

title: Learn about US Presidential Campaigns

title: Common Adverbs in Conversation: Amplifiers, Downtoners

title: Words Come and Go in English
Adverb and Adjective Choices in Conversation
summary: In today's Everyday Grammar, we will study how conversational grammar differs from written and formal grammatical structures. Americans often use adjective forms in place of adverbs. Americans also may use an adverb but choose not to say an –ly ending.

title: Reviewing Your Birthday Message

summary: English has many patterns. Learning and mastering these patterns can help you improve your writing and speaking skills. Today's common pattern is the transitive verb pattern. There are several types of transitive verbs, but they all have one thing in common: a direct object.

title: Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part Three
summary: What are intransitive verbs and how are they used? Find out in the third part of our series about common sentence patterns in the English language.

title: Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part Three

summary: In this week's report, we are going to explore one pattern that is commonly used in academic writing. Academic writers often use it to judge an idea. The structure we are exploring starts with a noun followed by a "that clause."

title: Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part One
summary: Today on Everyday Grammar, we explore two grammatical structures that you will see often in academic writing. These structures are used to evaluate ideas and evaluate actions.  Although common in academic writing, you might not want to use these structures in other types of writing.

title: Studying Sentence Patterns to Improve Your Writing, Part Two
summary: This week, we give you more information about a common sentence pattern in English: the linking verb pattern. Learning how to use linking verb patterns will help you improve your writing. You can think about creative ways to use them, or you can learn to recognize when you use them too often.

title: Studying Sentence Patterns to Improve Your Writing, Part One
summary: The English language has many patterns. By learning and understanding common sentence patterns, English learners can improve their writing skills.

title: Grammar and Christmas
summary: Christmas is full of pleasant surprises and surprising disappointments. Americans use certain words to show these emotions. Common examples include "even," "still," and "actually."
